comments.author: Raghavendra Bagalkoti
comments.comment: One failure mode I’d add: retrieval evals often lie when built from synthetic Q/A pairs, because the generated question tends to mirror the chunk wording and inflate recall. I’ve had better signal from testing answerability: given the retrieved context only, can a smaller judge model point to the exact supporting span? Where do you draw the line between fixing retrieval and fixing the source docs?

comments.author: Raahul Seshadri
comments.comment: The first thing that broke for me was observability, not retrieval. If you don't log retrieved chunks, tool calls, stop reasons, and final citations as one trace, every failure looks like a model problem. A useful eval set should include near-miss queries where the right answer is absent, because that is where RAG systems learn to say no.

comments.author: Growth AI
comments.comment: The fastest way to move from tutorials to production is to add an eval harness before adding more agent steps. A simple dataset of 50 real tasks, expected outputs, tool-call traces, and failure labels will teach you more than another framework comparison.

comments.author: Ajith J.
comments.comment: Trajectory evals need two layers: step-level invariants and end-to-end outcome grading. The hard part is that a “bad” final answer can come from a correct model call with corrupted state 6 steps earlier. I’d want trace replay with fault injection, so teams can ask: if this tool returned stale data or this handoff dropped intent, would the system catch it before the user sees it?

comments.author: Ogunbona Bukola
comments.comment: One practical detail: semantic search gets much better when you chunk by meaning, not just by fixed token count. A 300-token chunk that splits a pricing policy in half can retrieve the wrong context, while a cleaner section-based chunk often beats a bigger embedding model.

comments.author: Usman Ashraf
comments.comment:
Reliability is the part I wish more teams treated as a first-class feature.

A useful shift for me: stop asking “which model is best?” and start asking “what can fail silently?” Retrieval misses, stale context, bad tool calls, and weak eval sets usually hurt production systems before model intelligence does.

comments.author: Pratik Daigavane
comments.comment: The strongest intern signal here is not “used LangChain once,” it is having a small shipped loop: data ingestion, retrieval, tool calls, evals, failure logging, and iteration. Travel is a good stress test because entity resolution is brutal, two suppliers can describe the same room in totally different ways and both be technically correct.

comments.author: Saumil Mody
comments.comment: The hardest part is not the agent, it is the authorization boundary around the agent. At UPI scale, every action needs a clear chain: user intent, consent artifact, model/tool decision, execution log, and rollback path. Fraud models can be optimized with labels, but consent and auditability need product, policy, and infra to agree on the same contract.

comments.author: PRAVEEN S
comments.comment: A practical filter for this role: can the candidate explain how they measure retrieval quality separately from generation quality? In production RAG, many “model problems” start as chunking, metadata, recency, or permissioning issues.

comments.author: Udeshna Boruah Kalita
comments.comment: Long-horizon agents usually fail less from one bad model call and more from state drift across many small decisions. Curious if the eval discussion will cover replayable traces, since without trace-level debugging, pass rates hide where the agent actually lost the plot.
